item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi!

Thanks for the feedback, I really do appreciate it. It's honestly extremely helpful to hear what I can improve on going forward!

Just wanted to let you know that:

Spoilers
Close

You're right that information in the file likely shouldn't redacted. However, those redactions are intentionally written / left in by Dr. Gregory Werner, in an attempt to cover up his involvement with SCP-5516.

You bring up a fair point with the emails, and I'm going to think about that more and see if there's something more suitable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I somewhat agree with this. I also had a problem with the redactions and titles. While it’s important to write Safe, Easily containable, non-harmful SCPs (because the probability of all anomalies being serial killers is not realistic) the redaction makes no sense. Very rarely should a lot of classified information be put into a file that doesn’t need them. The anomaly itself is uninteresting (that’s not a bad thing per say) so it should not have a lot of things interesting enough to be worthy of redaction attached to it. Redaction happens after it’s written, not before. I hope that made sense, I’m on mobile.

The titles “scp” in the usernames and emails bother me a bit because from what I know “Secure, Contain, Protect” is sorta half in-universe and half out. This confuses it with “Special Containment Procedures” which is totally in-universe. I usually put “FND” for “Foundation”.

Not enough issues to warrant a -1 but not enough to make me +1. Good luck in future projects and I hoped my crit helped.

Oh and the story I find a tad bland or cliche.

I am not an experienced reviewer don’t sue me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll be honest, I didn't like this much. It's competently written, but feels like a loosely connected series of threads that does not tie together thematically or elegantly.

Using an otherwise self-contained anomaly to tell an interesting, human story is fine, and there are plenty articles that do it, but the way this is done leaves a lot on the table and scarcely elaborates further.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm so glad you enjoyed it! We should all care about the lobsters, especially the friendly ones.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Woah, you made me care about lobsters. Fuck.

Love the philosophical subtext and the idea of a simultaneously individual and collective awareness. Easy +1. Can't wait to see more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A huge thank you to all of those who took a look at this as an idea & draft, escpecially: InvictusRaged & MomBun (+ a third person in chat, whose name I feel awful for forgetting)!

It's been a while since I've been on the site, and I figured a bunch of lobster friends was a good way to get back into it.

If you're reading this, I hope you enjoyed it!

p.s. if anyone knows how collapsible #2 also opens collapsible #3, please let me know. I'd love to fix that but have no idea why it's happening.
